title: The Eternal Decree of God, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Eternal Decree of God_ Part 2 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Review: the eternal decree from Part 1
WCF 3.1 re-read: God ordained whatsoever comes to pass
Election: God chose people to go to heaven
Reprobation: God appointed others to judgment
These are God's sovereign purposes for salvation
Right understanding of election leads to greater confidence in God
Why we must begin with the decree of God before creation
summary: Second lesson in the four-part series on the eternal decree, focusing on election and reprobation (WCF Chapter 3). While some resist the doctrine of election, a right understanding of God's sovereign choice should produce greater confidence in the God who saved us. The lesson examines both election (God's choice of some for salvation) and reprobation (God's appointment of others for judgment) as aspects of God's eternal decree, showing how they flow logically after the creation decree.
transcript:
This morning our goal is to talk about election — the idea that God sovereignly chose some people to go to heaven and passed over others. The part about passing over others is the doctrine of reprobation — the idea that God has appointed some to judgment. These are God's sovereign purposes. And even though some people resist the doctrine of election, a right understanding of it leads us to greater confidence in the God who saved us.

## Review of Last Week

Before we get there, let us review what we saw last week. If you have your hymnals, turn to page 850. We are going to read together paragraph 1 of chapter 3, which says:

> *"God from all eternity did, by the most wise and holy counsel of His own will, freely and unchangeably ordain whatsoever comes to pass; yet so as thereby neither is God the author of sin, nor is violence offered to the will of the creatures, nor is the liberty or contingency of second causes taken away, but rather established."*

We only looked at that one element of chapter 3 last week. We saw that we must begin with the decree of God because everything flows from it. The Confession is organized very logically: chapter 3 is on the decree of God — that God purposed everything to happen. Chapter 4 is creation, one of the works of God. Chapter 5 is providence. Election is not an isolated doctrine. It follows from the fact that God decreed everything that happens. It is a particular application of the idea of the decree outlined in paragraph 1.

The big idea from last week: God from all eternity, by his most wise and holy counsel, freely and unchangeably ordained whatever comes to pass, without being the author of sin and without destroying human responsibility.

Key elements: God's decree is eternal — before creation. God didn't react to things as they happened. The decree is wise, holy, and free — nothing forced him to do it. God ordains whatever comes to pass. Now remember: this is a hindsight doctrine. We don't know what God has decreed until it has happened. That is what distinguishes this view from fatalism. Islam is fatalistic. Hinduism is fatalistic. If you're born poor, you're not supposed to be helped because karma ordained that for you. If someone is drowning, it's because fate determined that this person would die by drowning. We don't see life that way. We know that God has ordained things because the Bible teaches us so, and we know what God has ordained once it has happened.

We also saw that God is not the author — not the cause, originator, or one responsible — of sin. That is what the Confession means by "author." Even though God appoints all things, we are still responsible for our actions, and secondary causes remain intact. God also appoints the means by which things are going to happen. You have heard the joke about the man in the flood who sat on top of his house and when a boat came by said, "No thanks, I'm waiting for God to rescue me." Then a helicopter came, and again he refused. He drowned. When he got to heaven he said, "Lord, you never delivered me!" And God said, "What are you talking about? I sent a boat and a helicopter and you refused to get on." God also appoints the means by which he accomplishes his purposes.

A critical takeaway from last week: God does not react to history. History unfolds according to God's eternal purpose. We sometimes picture God's sovereignty as a heavenly cat herder, where all of us are going in different directions and he's sovereign by reacting to us. That is not how God is sovereign. God is sovereign because he has decreed whatever comes to pass — he is simply not reacting.

So last week answered the question: does God have an eternal plan for all things? The question this week is: how does that eternal plan apply specifically to salvation and judgment? Election and reprobation are not separate decrees of God — they are expressions of the one decree concerning angels and men that God decreed in all eternity.

## The Big Idea

Here is the big idea for today: before the foundation of the world, God sovereignly chose some unto everlasting life and passed over others — all for his glory. This flows directly from Westminster Confession of Faith 3:1. If God ordains whatever comes to pass, then salvation itself must rest on his decree.

Many people have no problem with the idea that God ordains whatever happens in general. But they don't accept the logical application of that in their daily lives. We are fine with God's sovereignty in the abstract, but not where it intersects our own lives — especially the lives of our unsaved loved ones. I hope that by the time we are done you will see that this is actually a hopeful doctrine, not a discouraging one.

## Unconditional Election: WCF Chapter 3, Paragraphs 3 and 5

The main thing we are talking about this morning is unconditional election. Look at paragraphs 3 and 5 of chapter 3 of the Confession.

Paragraph 3 says: "By the decree of God, for the manifestation of His glory, some men and angels are predestinated unto everlasting life, and others foreordained to everlasting death."

Paragraph 5 says: "Those of mankind that are predestinated unto life, God, before the foundation of the world was laid, according to His eternal and immutable purpose, and the secret counsel and good pleasure of His will, hath chosen in Christ, unto everlasting glory, out of His mere free grace and love, without any foresight of faith or good works, or perseverance in either of them, or any other thing in the creature, as conditions or causes moving Him thereunto; and all to the praise of His glorious grace."

In a nutshell: God's choosing of who is going to be saved has nothing to do with us and everything to do with him. That is offensive to the natural heart — the idea that you are not special, that God did not look at you and say, "I need that person, I want that one on my team." That is not what the Bible teaches. It has nothing to do with who we are. I hope that as we see this, it will actually encourage you — that salvation has nothing to do with who we are.

**What does unconditional election mean?** It means this: God chose some unto everlasting life from all eternity, out of mere grace and love, not based on foreseen faith, works, or merit. Why did God choose you? Because he wanted to. That sometimes doesn't feel like a satisfying answer, but that is the answer we have.

Ephesians 1:4–6:

> *"...just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, by which He made us accepted in the Beloved."* (Ephesians 1:4–6)

The elect were chosen before the foundation of the world, according to the purpose of his will, to the praise of his glorious grace. That is what Paul teaches in Ephesians 1.

Romans 9:11–18 also teaches this:

> *"...for the children not yet being born, nor having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works but of Him who calls, it was said to her, 'The older shall serve the younger.' As it is written, 'Jacob I have loved, but Esau I have hated.' What shall we say then? Is there unrighteousness with God? Certainly not! For He says to Moses, 'I will have mercy on whomever I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whomever I will have compassion.' So then it is not of him who wills, nor of him who runs, but of God who shows mercy."* (Romans 9:11–16)

God's purpose of election stands. Nothing deviates it. It is not because of works but because of him who calls. Election is not an exception to God's sovereignty — it is the most merciful display of his sovereignty. If God had not purposed in eternity to save a people and had left it to themselves, nobody would be saved. Election is a display of his mercy.

## The Canons of Dort and TULIP

Let me look at unconditional election in more detail with the help of the first canon of Dort. Note: a canon is not a cannon — not a war device. A canon is a rule or statement of doctrine.

In 1618, there was a controversy in the church in Holland. A group of pastors brought a complaint to the prince of the time, saying that ministers should not be required to believe that humanity was completely affected by sin, that those who come to faith could lose their salvation, that Christ died specifically for particular people, that God chose people independently of anything they do, or that God's grace is irresistible. The prince called an ecumenical council, with delegations from many nations, to examine the Scriptures on these five issues.

The council took place in the city of Dordrecht — in English sometimes called Dort. The results became known as the Canons of Dort. There were five canons, because they addressed five doctrines. The first doctrine tackled was unconditional election.

You may have heard the acronym TULIP: Total depravity, Unconditional election, Limited atonement, Irresistible grace, Perseverance of the saints. The original order, however, was not TULIP but ULDIP — because that is the order in which the Synod of Dort addressed the issues.

The first canon says election is the immutable — meaning unchangeable — purpose of God, by which he chose to salvation in Christ, from the whole human race, a fixed multitude of particular persons. A specific number that neither shrinks nor grows. This plan, this purpose, does not change. The number of people elected to salvation does not change.

A lot of us struggle with the idea that this is a small number — but it is not a small number. Genesis 17 records the Abrahamic covenant: the offspring of Abraham will be more numerous than the stars of the heavens or the grains of sand on the seashores. And Galatians 3 tells us that if you are in Christ, you are Abraham's seed and an heir according to the promise. And Revelation 7 gives us this vision:

> *"After these things I looked, and behold, a great multitude which no one could number, of all nations, tribes, peoples, and tongues, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, clothed with white robes, with palm branches in their hands, and crying out with a loud voice, saying, 'Salvation belongs to our God who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb!'"* (Revelation 7:9–10)

This is the description of the triumphant church in heaven — the universal church, not the visible church on earth. Spurgeon believed, based on the character of God as revealed in Scripture, that there would be more people in heaven than in hell, because God reveals himself more often as the God of mercy than the God of wrath. That is speculation, of course, but it is grounded in how God chooses to reveal himself.

The Canons continue to teach that God's election is from before the foundation of the world — it is not something continuing today but something God did before creation. When God elected people, he was already treating them as fallen creatures. The only criterion God used in choosing people to salvation was his own will — not anything in the ones being elected. God chose his elect according to the most free and good pleasure of his will, out of mere grace. Those chosen were neither better nor more worthy than others but were like others fallen into common misery.

The first Canon also teaches that God elected people not only to be saved from their sin but to grow in Christ and to be glorified in Christ when he returns. God is not just after saved people — he is after disciples. From eternity, God made Christ the mediator and head of all the elect and the foundation of salvation. He decreed to give them true faith in Christ, to justify them, sanctify them, and finally, after powerfully preserving them in communion with his Son, to glorify them.

## Ephesians 1: Predestination Is a Matter of Praise

Close your hymnals and open your Bible to Ephesians 1. These verses are important because they teach us that predestination is a matter of praise — not something we put up with or talk about in hushed tones, but a matter of praise.

> *"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love."* (Ephesians 1:3–4)

Some people hate this doctrine — and hate is not too strong a word. But for those who allow the Scriptures to shape their thinking, this is a glorious doctrine. Paul describes predestination as a spiritual blessing worthy of the character of heaven itself. For Paul, the fact that God the Father planned to save his people in and through Christ is a matter of joy, comfort, and praise.

The word translated "blessed" at the beginning of verse 3 is not the usual word for blessing — it is a compound meaning "to speak well." Paul says: I speak well of God. I praise God for predestining a people to himself. And note: Paul speaks well of God because God first spoke well of us in election.

The word translated "world" in "before the foundation of the world" is not the word for earth. It is the word for the created order — cosmos. You may remember Carl Sagan's television program. What was it called? *Cosmos.* That is this word. Before there was a created order, before the first atom was called into existence, God spoke well of us in planning how he was going to save us.

As best as we can tell from Ephesians 1, Romans 9, and related passages, the logical process in the mind of God concerning our salvation — not the historical order, but the logical order — went like this. First, God determined to create the universe. Second, in that universe, he determined to create humanity. Third, he determined to choose some people for himself and to leave others to themselves. That is what Paul means in Romans 9:21 when he says: "Does not the potter have power over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor?" Fourth, he determined how he was going to make those he had chosen to be his people — by planning the fall and the redemption to be accomplished by the Son and applied by the Spirit.

This view is called Supralapsarianism — *supra* meaning "before" and *laps* meaning "fall" — the view that God chose a people before the fall was even in view. Add that to your theological vocabulary.

This plan was the best and most perfect plan ever — because God came up with it. And God's plan culminates with people like you and me becoming sons and daughters of God.

God's plan executed by the Spirit also gives us an identity and a way of life. We are not just elected into heaven — we are elected into holiness. Look at verse 4:

> *"...just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before Him in love."* (Ephesians 1:4)

Holiness is both who we are and what we do. It is both identity and practice. Election is in Christ and because of Christ — nothing to do with who we are. In this sense it is unconditional. There is nothing in us that conditions God's choosing us to himself. Chapter 2 of Ephesians begins by telling us that apart from Christ we are dead. The only thing dead people contribute is their smell.

## Reprobation

Now let us talk about reprobation — the idea that God appoints people to judgment. If people have a hard time with the positive side of election, this is even more difficult.

Some prefer the language of "permission" or "allowance," as if that removes God's involvement. But if you have the power to stop something from happening and you choose not to use it, you are still accountable. The language of permission or allowance does not change the reality.

The Bible does teach that God sends people to judgment. Matthew 25 gives us the parable of the sheep and the goats. The same king who says to those on his right, "Come, you blessed of My Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world" (v. 34) actively says to those on his left, "Depart from Me" — and commands them into eternal fire (v. 41). He does not merely walk away and leave them. He actively pronounces judgment.

1 Thessalonians 5:9: "For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ." We are not of those appointed to wrath. The implication is clear: there are some whom God appointed to wrath.

Jude 4: "For certain men have crept in unnoticed, who long ago were marked out for this condemnation." When was the judgment appointed? Long ago.

Romans 9:19–24:

> *"You will say to me then, 'Why does He still find fault? For who has resisted His will?' But indeed, O man, who are you to reply against God? Will the thing formed say to him who formed it, 'Why have you made me like this?' Does not the potter have power over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor? What if God, wanting to show His wrath and to make His power known, endured with much longsuffering the vessels of wrath prepared for destruction, and that He might make known the riches of His glory on the vessels of mercy, which He had prepared beforehand for glory, even us whom He called, not of the Jews only, but also of the Gentiles?"* (Romans 9:19–24)

God prepared some for honor and some for dishonor — not because of anything in them, but according to his sovereign will. In all of this, ultimately, it is for God's glory and for our enjoyment. The chief end of man is to glorify God and enjoy him forever. So the chief end of election is to glorify God and that we have joy in God through it.

So if week one taught us that God ordains all things wisely and holy, then this lesson teaches us that God saves sinners graciously and justly according to that same decree.

## Seven Applications

**First, humility.** There is no room to boast in our salvation.

**Second, assurance.** Salvation rests in God's eternal purpose. We on our best day would not be good enough for God to choose us, and on our worst day are not so bad that God would refuse us. Assurance rests entirely in him.

**Third, evangelism.** All you Reformed folks who supposedly don't believe in evangelism — that is a misunderstanding of history. The greatest missionaries in history have been Reformed. God ordains both ends and means: the proclamation of the gospel will result in people being saved. If you believe the doctrine of sovereignty in salvation, you know that when you proclaim the gospel, people will get saved.

**Fourth, worship.** When we see who God is, it drops us to our knees in worship.

**Fifth, thankfulness.** If God hadn't chosen us, we would never have known him.

**Sixth, holiness.** If you believe in Jesus, it must show itself — because you are elected unto holiness.

**Seventh, love.** God loved you even before you were. Even before you existed. And that love should be our response.

But at the end of the day, brothers and sisters: we do not preach election. We preach Christ and him crucified. That is the means God uses to save people.
